questions.text: Under the Single Audit Act, which federal entity is responsible for establishing audit requirements for state and local government recipients of federal financial assistance?
options: A. The Office of Management and Budget (OMB)
B. The Government Accountability Office (GAO)
C. The Department of Justice (DOJ)
D. The Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration (TIGTA)

questions.text: What is the legal term for the fraudulent practice whereby a government employee uses their official position to solicit or accept anything of value in exchange for official action?
options: A. Honest services fraud
B. Bribery of public officials
C. Extortion under color of official right
D. Kickback conspiracy

questions.text: In government procurement fraud investigations, what does 'cost shifting' specifically refer to?
options: A. The allocation of costs from commercial contracts to government contracts to increase profit
B. The movement of funds between budget line items without proper authorization
C. The temporary relocation of assets to avoid asset-based audits
D. The transfer of liability for contract performance to subcontractors

questions.text: Which statutory provision allows private citizens with knowledge of false claims submitted to the government to file lawsuits on behalf of the government and potentially recover damages?
options: A. The Whistleblower Protection Act of 1989
B. The qui tam provision of the False Claims Act
C. The Government Employee Whistleblower Act
D. The Federal Tort Claims Act

questions.text: In Social Security fraud investigations, what is meant by the term 'overpayment due to continuing eligibility'?
options: A. Beneficiaries receiving duplicate payments through system errors
B. Benefits paid after a beneficiary's death because eligibility changes were not processed timely
C. Intentional misrepresentation of income to qualify for higher benefit amounts
D. Multiple agency payments for the same qualifying condition

questions.text: What distinguishes 'invoice manipulation' from 'billing fraud' in government contract fraud?
options: A. Invoice manipulation is civil in nature while billing fraud is always criminal
B. Invoice manipulation involves falsifying specific line items on legitimate invoices, whereas billing fraud encompasses submitting invoices for work never performed
C. Billing fraud requires intent while invoice manipulation can occur through negligence
D. Invoice manipulation is limited to defense contractors while billing fraud applies to all government vendors

questions.text: Under the Anti-Deficiency Act, which of the following constitutes a violation?
options: A. Obligating or expending funds in excess of an appropriation or apportionment
B. Failing to segregate duties within a government accounting office
C. Accepting unsolicited proposals from private contractors
D. Delaying the payment of legitimate invoices beyond 30 days

questions.text: What is the primary distinction between 'vendor fraud' and 'supplier collusion' in municipal procurement?
options: A. Vendor fraud involves single entities while supplier collusion requires coordination among multiple entities
B. Vendor fraud is always criminal while supplier collusion is only civil
C. Vendor fraud targets the procurement process while supplier collusion targets contract performance
D. Vendor fraud cannot occur in competitive bidding situations

questions.text: In the context of government grant fraud, what does 'matching fund falsification' specifically mean?
options: A. Overstating the value of resources or cash contributions required to be provided by the grant recipient
B. Using federal grant funds to match another federal grant from a different agency
C. Failing to document the source of matching funds provided by the grant recipient
D. Transferring matching fund requirements between different program years

questions.text: Which of the following best defines 'double-dipping' in the context of federal employee benefits fraud?
options: A. Claiming unemployment benefits while employed
B. Receiving benefits from multiple federal agencies for the same loss or condition
C. Collecting retirement annuity payments while simultaneously receiving disability benefits for the same condition
D. Filing duplicate claims within the same federal program

questions.text: In cybersecurity fraud involving government systems, what is 'credential harvesting' most accurately defined as?
options: A. The systematic collection of usernames and passwords through phishing or social engineering to gain unauthorized access to government systems
B. The legal audit process used to verify employee access permissions
C. The temporary suspension of user accounts during security investigations
D. The encryption of sensitive government data to prevent unauthorized access

questions.text: What is the principal purpose of the 'Prompt Payment Act' in government procurement?
options: A. To establish criminal penalties for contractors who submit false invoices
B. To require federal agencies to pay contractors within a specified timeframe and pay interest penalties for late payments
C. To mandate competitive bidding for all government contracts
D. To establish audit requirements for contractor accounting systems

questions.text: In healthcare fraud involving Medicare, what does 'unbundling of services' refer to?
options: A. Combining multiple service codes into a single billing code to reduce reimbursement
B. Deliberately separating related services that should be billed together as a single comprehensive code to increase reimbursement
C. Requesting separate authorizations for different components of treatment
D. Billing services in a different order than they were actually provided to patients

questions.text: A CFE is distinguishing between embezzlement and misappropriation of public funds in a city treasurer's office. Which characteristic BEST differentiates embezzlement from other forms of asset misappropriation in government contexts?
options: A. Embezzlement requires the offender to have fiduciary responsibility and access to assets through their position of trust
B. Embezzlement only applies to cash transactions, while misappropriation can involve any asset type
C. Embezzlement is a civil violation, whereas misappropriation of public funds is exclusively criminal
D. Embezzlement occurs exclusively at the federal level, while misappropriation is limited to local government

questions.text: When comparing gratuities versus bribes in government procurement fraud, what is the PRIMARY legal distinction that a CFE must understand for investigative purposes?
options: A. Gratuities are gifts offered after a contract decision, while bribes are offered in exchange for future official action
B. Bribes involve only monetary consideration, while gratuities can include non-monetary benefits
C. Gratuities require written documentation to constitute fraud, whereas bribes can be verbal
D. Bribes are illegal under federal law only, while gratuities violate state ethics regulations exclusively

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing two government contract scenarios: Scheme A involves a contractor submitting identical bids across three separate procurements, and Scheme B involves competitors meeting before bid submission to agree on pricing. How should these schemes be differentiated?
options: A. Scheme A is collusion while Scheme B is bid rigging; they represent fundamentally different conspiracy structures
B. Scheme A is bid rigging and Scheme B is collusion; collusion precedes and enables bid rigging activities
C. Both schemes are identical violations; the distinction depends only on the number of contractors involved
D. Scheme A violates antitrust law while Scheme B violates only procurement regulations, not competition law

questions.text: In distinguishing between time theft and ghost employment in a public sector payroll fraud investigation, which factor is MOST critical for classification?
options: A. The number of hours fraudulently claimed distinguishes between the two schemes
B. Time theft involves an employee falsifying hours worked while ghost employment involves payment for non-existent employees
C. Ghost employment only affects government benefits while time theft impacts salary accounts
D. Time theft is a federal violation while ghost employment is prosecuted solely under state labor laws

questions.text: A CFE is evaluating two public benefit schemes: Scheme X involves a beneficiary failing to report income changes, and Scheme Y involves a government employee deliberately processing fraudulent applications. What distinguishes these as different categories of benefit fraud?
options: A. Scheme X is claimant fraud while Scheme Y is internal fraud; they differ in perpetrator status and administrative control
B. Scheme X involves larger dollar amounts while Scheme Y affects fewer total recipients
C. Scheme Y can only be prosecuted criminally while Scheme X allows only civil remedies
D. Scheme X targets federal programs while Scheme Y exclusively impacts state-administered benefits

questions.text: When comparing change order fraud versus scope creep in government construction contracts, how should a CFE differentiate between intentional fraud and legitimate project modifications?
options: A. Change order fraud involves intentional misrepresentation to justify unnecessary work authorization, while scope creep represents undocumented but reasonable project expansion
B. Both represent the same violation; the distinction is merely administrative
C. Change order fraud only applies to federal projects exceeding $1 million in value
D. Scope creep always requires change orders while fraud can occur without any documentation

questions.text: A CFE is investigating two grant fraud scenarios: Scenario 1 involves a nonprofit inflating indirect cost rates, and Scenario 2 involves a nonprofit submitting a grant application while ineligible. How are these fundamentally different?
options: A. Scenario 1 is cost allocation fraud while Scenario 2 is eligibility fraud; they target different program requirements
B. Scenario 1 is civil fraud while Scenario 2 is exclusively criminal fraud
C. Scenario 2 requires proving intent while Scenario 1 is a strict liability violation
D. Both scenarios violate identical statutory provisions and require identical investigative approaches

questions.text: When distinguishing between invoice fraud and kickback schemes in government contracting, which element is the KEY differentiator?
options: A. Invoice fraud involves false billing documents, while kickbacks involve undisclosed financial benefits flowing back to influence official decisions
B. Kickbacks only occur in defense contracting while invoice fraud affects all government contracts
C. Invoice fraud requires multiple false invoices while kickbacks can occur from a single transaction
D. Kickbacks are always discovered through document analysis while invoice fraud requires witness testimony

questions.text: A CFE is comparing two procurement violations: Violation A involves a vendor splitting contracts to avoid competitive bidding thresholds, and Violation B involves a procuring official accepting a consulting contract from a vendor they previously approved. What distinguishes these violations?
options: A. Violation A is procurement fraud while Violation B is a conflicts of interest and post-employment ethics violation
B. Violation A requires proving financial gain while Violation B only requires showing the relationship existed
C. Violation A is prosecuted criminally while Violation B results only in contract cancellation
D. Both violations are identical and prosecuted under the same fraud statute

questions.text: When investigating property tax assessment fraud, how does fraudulent over-assessment differ from fraudulent under-assessment in terms of perpetrator motivation and detection difficulty?
options: A. Over-assessment benefits the taxing authority and is harder to detect; under-assessment benefits property owners and typically generates complaints
B. Under-assessment is always intentional while over-assessment can be accidental
C. Both schemes involve identical perpetrators and motivation structures
D. Over-assessment only violates state law while under-assessment violates federal regulations

questions.text: A CFE is distinguishing between license plate theft and vehicle registration fraud in a state DMV department. Which characteristic BEST differentiates these two schemes?
options: A. License plate theft involves physical asset taking, while registration fraud involves falsifying documents to create unauthorized vehicle records
B. License plate theft only affects individual citizens while registration fraud impacts commercial fleets
C. Registration fraud requires organized crime involvement while license plate theft is a simple property crime
D. Both schemes are identical violations with different procedural remedies

questions.text: When comparing travel expense fraud to per diem abuse by government employees, what is the ESSENTIAL distinction for investigative classification?
options: A. Travel expense fraud involves false submissions for transportation and lodging costs, while per diem abuse involves claiming meal/incidental allowances without legitimate travel
B. Travel expense fraud requires false receipts while per diem abuse can occur with authentic documentation
C. Per diem abuse only occurs during out-of-state travel while travel expense fraud can involve domestic expenses
D. Both violations are investigated using identical methodologies with no meaningful distinction

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing two environmental compliance fraud schemes: Scheme Alpha involves a contractor certifying compliance with environmental standards without conducting required testing, and Scheme Beta involves a government inspector receiving unreported payments to overlook documented violations. How should these be differentiated?
options: A. Scheme Alpha is certification fraud by a contractor; Scheme Beta is corruption fraud by a government official—they differ in perpetrator type and mechanism of deception
B. Both schemes involve identical violations of environmental law prosecuted under the same statute
C. Scheme Alpha involves only civil penalties while Scheme Beta is exclusively criminal
D. Scheme Beta requires proving financial gain while Scheme Alpha only requires showing non-compliance

questions.text: A CFE is examining a state transportation department where supervisors are systematically approving invoices for road maintenance work that was never performed. The invoices total $4.7 million over 18 months. Which documentation would be MOST critical to establish the fraudulent scheme?
options: A. Supervisor approval logs and email confirmations alone
B. Site inspection reports, GPS records, and photographic evidence compared against invoice dates
C. Verbal statements from contractors about project completion
D. Budget allocation records showing funds were available

questions.text: During an investigation of a county health department, a CFE discovers that a contracted medical supplier inflated unit prices by 340% compared to identical products purchased by neighboring counties. What is the PRIMARY investigative challenge in this scenario?
options: A. Determining whether price differences constitute fraud or legitimate vendor differentiation
B. Proving that the procurement officer intentionally selected the overpriced vendor
C. Establishing if comparative market analysis was required by procurement policy
D. Identifying whether the supplier provided kickbacks to specific individuals

questions.text: A municipal employee submitted 47 reimbursement requests for travel expenses over 14 months, each just under the $500 threshold requiring secondary approval. Analysis shows the trips were fabricated. Which control failure BEST explains how this fraud persisted?
options: A. Absence of annual expense audits
B. Inadequate segregation of duties in the approval process
C. Threshold-based controls without cumulative review or pattern analysis
D. Insufficient training on travel policy documentation requirements

questions.text: A CFE investigating a state pension fund discovers that a benefits administrator processed early retirement approvals for 12 ineligible employees in exchange for cash payments. The administrator claims the employees misrepresented their service records. What evidence would MOST directly establish the administrator's culpability?
options: A. Documentation that service records were altered after the eligibility reviews
B. Financial records showing deposits to the administrator's personal accounts coinciding with approval dates
C. Statements from the beneficiaries admitting they falsified information
D. Comparison of pension payment amounts to industry standards

questions.text: A state environmental agency contracted with a private firm to conduct water quality testing at 156 locations. The CFE's analysis reveals that 89% of the test results were statistically identical across vastly different geographic areas and pollution risk levels. What does this pattern primarily suggest?
options: A. The testing methodology was scientifically superior to standard protocols
B. The contractor likely fabricated results rather than conducting genuine testing
C. Environmental conditions across the state were unusually uniform
D. The agency's sampling procedures were properly standardized

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing a federal grant program where a nonprofit submitted inflated indirect cost rates (52% vs. the allowed 26%) for five consecutive years. The organization claims this was an accounting interpretation error. Which finding would BEST challenge this defense?
options: A. Prior audit findings recommending rate adjustments
B. Evidence that senior management received bonuses calculated using the inflated rates
C. Documentation showing the CFO received formal training on grant cost allocation requirements
D. Correspondence indicating the nonprofit declined to participate in cost verification audits

questions.text: During an investigation of a city housing authority, a CFE discovers that a manager steered $2.1 million in maintenance contracts exclusively to a company owned by the manager's spouse. The contracts passed technical reviews and were completed on schedule. Which factor would make prosecution MOST difficult?
options: A. The spouse's company technically performed satisfactory work
B. The manager delegated final approval to an assistant
C. Documentation proving intentional deception in the selection process
D. The presence of competitive bidding documentation in the file

questions.text: A CFE investigating a state motor vehicle department discovers that three employees created 186 fraudulent driver's licenses for individuals who never took required tests. The employees retained all license fees ($18,600 total). Which characteristic makes this scheme particularly concerning from a public sector perspective?
options: A. The monetary loss was relatively modest
B. Public safety risk and breach of government certifying function
C. The employees acted independently without supervisory knowledge
D. The fraudulent licenses were eventually detected through random audits

questions.text: A CFE is examining a county judicial system where a contracted court reporting service billed 8,400 hours annually for a facility with 5 courtrooms operating 2,000 billable hours yearly maximum. When confronted, the vendor claims the contract allows for 'administrative and preparatory work' billing. What is the MOST appropriate investigative response?
options: A. Accept the explanation and close the investigation
B. Request detailed time records, cross-reference with court schedules, and interview court personnel
C. Calculate damages based on the hourly rate and submit a civil claim
D. Refer the matter to local law enforcement for criminal charges

questions.text: A state education department grants $850,000 annually to a school district for technology training. A CFE's investigation reveals that purchased equipment (laptop computers) was immediately resold to a for-profit company at 60% below cost, with proceeds deposited to a school foundation account controlled by the superintendent's family member. What is the PRIMARY fraud classification?
options: A. Simple embezzlement of grant funds
B. Asset misappropriation with grant compliance violation
C. Fraudulent grant application with false certifications
D. Kickback scheme between the school and equipment vendor

questions.text: A CFE investigating a municipal water utility discovers that the finance director altered meter readings in the billing system, reducing charges for a commercial customer by $127,000 over 36 months. The customer is the director's former employer. The director claims the rate reduction was verbally authorized by the department head. What documentation would be ESSENTIAL to prove intentional fraud?
options: A. Email confirmation from the department head authorizing the specific rate reduction
B. Evidence of the unauthorized system access method and lack of approval trail documentation
C. Testimony that the customer paid the falsified invoices without question
D. Comparative rate analysis showing other customers received standard pricing

questions.text: During an investigation of a state corrections department, a CFE identifies that a food service contractor submitted identical invoices for meals provided on state holidays when facilities operated at minimum capacity. The invoices claimed full-capacity meal provision (3,200 meals) despite documented staffing indicating only 800 inmates present. What type of control would have BEST prevented this scheme?
options: A. Random audits of food service operations
B. Requirement for independent verification of inmate population counts matching invoice quantities
C. Annual contract renegotiation with competitive rebidding
D. Increased supplier communication protocols

questions.text: A CFE is assisting a state attorney general investigating a Medicaid provider fraud scheme. A clinic submitted 12,400 claims for physical therapy services at $185 per session (total $2.3 million). Medical records show 8,600 legitimate visits. The provider claims billing errors in their electronic system. Which evidence would MOST clearly establish intentional fraud versus system error?
options: A. The specific error pattern affecting predominantly high-reimbursement codes
B. System audit logs showing manual intervention and override of validation controls
C. Provider's refusal to accept a partial refund settlement
D. Complaints from patients about phantom therapy sessions

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a state welfare department where caseworkers systematically approved benefits for ineligible applicants in exchange for kickbacks. The scheme involved 487 fraudulent cases over 18 months, totaling $2.1 million in improper disbursements. Which investigative approach would be MOST effective in establishing both the systematic nature and the corrupt intent?
options: A. Reviewing only the most recent 50 cases to establish a pattern, as statistical sampling is sufficient for prosecutorial purposes
B. Analyzing transaction flows, communication records between caseworkers and beneficiaries, and comparing approval rates against departmental benchmarks
C. Conducting interviews with caseworkers first to obtain confessions before examining documentary evidence
D. Focusing exclusively on the monetary amounts involved, as dollar value alone demonstrates systematic fraud

questions.text: During an audit of a municipal pension fund, a CFE discovers that the fund's investment manager directed $15 million to a private equity firm owned by the fund's board chairman's nephew, despite the firm having no relevant experience. The investments have underperformed by 8% annually compared to benchmarks. What is the PRIMARY fraud classification for this conduct?
options: A. Embezzlement, since the fund manager directly controlled fund assets
B. Conflict of interest and breach of fiduciary duty, with potential self-dealing fraud
C. Securities fraud under federal regulations due to the underperformance
D. Money laundering, as funds were transferred to a related entity

questions.text: A CFE is examining a state transportation department contract where a contractor submitted invoices for 2,400 labor hours on a highway resurfacing project. Physical site inspections and GPS tracking data suggest only 1,600 hours of actual work occurred. The contractor argues that the additional 800 hours represent 'project management and coordination' not directly chargeable to site work. Under government contract law, how should the CFE classify this discrepancy?
options: A. As an accounting disagreement requiring only contract renegotiation
B. As fraudulent overbilling only if the contractor intentionally falsified timesheets
C. As potential fraud through false statements on invoices, as submitted claims must accurately represent work performed
D. As standard industry practice that requires no further investigation

questions.text: A city comptroller authorizes payment to a vendor for 50,000 units of office supplies at $8 per unit ($400,000 total), but the goods received were only 35,000 units. When questioned, the vendor's owner claims the invoice was prepared by their accounting department and they assumed the city's receiving department verified quantities. Which party bears PRIMARY liability for the false claim?
options: A. Only the vendor, as they submitted the invoice
B. Only the city, as they authorized payment without verification
C. The vendor primarily, as they submitted a false invoice regardless of internal procedures
D. Neither party, as this constitutes a clerical error rather than fraud

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a federal grant administrator approved $4.7 million in research funding to a university despite the Principal Investigator failing to disclose a significant financial relationship with a pharmaceutical company whose products were the subject of the research. The administrator was not aware of the undisclosed relationship. What is the PRIMARY violation?
options: A. Grant fraud by the university, as the conflict of interest constitutes a material misrepresentation in the grant application
B. No violation occurred, as the administrator was unaware of the conflict
C. Fraud only against the pharmaceutical company, not the government
D. A civil contract dispute requiring only grant suspension, not criminal investigation

questions.text: A CFE examining a state licensing board discovers that an official approved professional licenses for 23 applicants who failed required examinations. Investigation reveals the official received monetary gifts totaling $180,000 from the applicants' families over an 8-month period. The official claims these were 'personal loans' unrelated to licensing decisions. What evidence would be MOST critical to establish corrupt intent?
options: A. Testimony from the applicants confirming they made payments

B. Temporal proximity analysis showing gifts correlating with exam failures being overlooked, absence of repayment documentation, and the official's financial records showing no legitimate loan purpose
C. The total dollar amount of $180,000, as any amount above $100,000 presumes corruption
D. Written agreements between the official and applicants explicitly exchanging money for licenses

questions.text: A county social services director implemented a policy requiring caseworkers to process child welfare cases within 72 hours regardless of investigation completion. A CFE discovers that 34% of opened cases were fraudulently closed without proper investigation to meet this deadline, resulting in at least 12 children remaining in unsafe situations. Who should be investigated for potential fraud?
options: A. Only the individual caseworkers who closed cases improperly
B. Only the director, as the policy created the fraudulent environment
C. Both the director (for creating systematic conditions enabling fraud) and caseworkers (for executing the fraudulent closures)
D. No one, as this represents operational inefficiency rather than fraud

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a state environmental agency where officials approved air quality variance permits for manufacturing facilities in exchange for campaign contributions to elected officials' re-election campaigns. The contributions were made through legitimate registered PACs, but communications show explicit quid pro quo agreements. What is the PRIMARY challenge in prosecuting this conduct?
options: A. Campaign contributions are always protected political speech and cannot constitute fraud
B. The quid pro quo agreement for official action in exchange for contributions constitutes bribery and honest services fraud, distinguishable from protected political activity
C. Only monetary bribes constitute fraud; campaign contributions are categorically excluded
D. The involvement of PACs makes individual criminal liability impossible to establish

questions.text: A municipal procurement officer received sealed bids for a water treatment equipment contract. Before the official bid opening, the officer provided an advanced copy of one competitor's bid to a favored contractor, who then submitted a bid $5,000 lower but with inflated change order provisions. The favored contractor ultimately won and received $340,000 in approved change orders. How should this scheme be classified?
options: A. As unethical but not fraudulent, since the final contract amount was competitively determined
B. As bid rigging and fraud, involving advance disclosure of confidential bid information that compromised competitive integrity
C. As a change order management issue unrelated to the initial bidding process
D. As fraud only if the original contract amount itself exceeded the fair market value

questions.text: A state university's financial aid director approved $8.9 million in institutional grants to 347 students, later discovering that 89 students (25.6%) did not meet citizenship requirements mandated by federal guidelines. The director claims the verification system was outdated and responsibility falls on the federal government for inadequate oversight. What is the director's PRIMARY liability?
options: A. No liability, as federal oversight failure absolves institutional responsibility
B. Institutional liability only; individual liability cannot attach without proving intentional knowledge
C. Individual liability for breach of fiduciary duty and potential grant fraud, as institutional officials are responsible for compliance with grant conditions
D. Only civil liability; criminal prosecution requires proof the director deliberately admitted ineligible students

questions.text: A CFE investigating a municipal parking authority discovers that the director authorized $12 million in parking violation citations against vehicles belonging to the director's ex-spouse's business competitors over a 3-year period. Examination of citation records shows no legitimate traffic violations occurred. The director claims prosecutorial discretion in enforcement decisions. How should this conduct be characterized?
options: A. As an appropriate exercise of prosecutorial discretion requiring no investigation
B. As political retaliation that, while improper, does not constitute fraud against the government
C. As misuse of official position and potential fraud, as false citations constitute false claims against the government and violation of civil rights
D. As a personal dispute between the director and competitors unrelated to government functions
questions.tag: Misuse of Official Authority & Government Resources

questions.text: A CFE is examining a state pharmacy benefits manager's payment system for Medicaid prescriptions. Analysis reveals that for 12,000 prescriptions, the PBM submitted claims for brand-name medications but actually dispensed generic equivalents, retaining the price differential ($8.4 million annually). The PBM argues that substitution benefits patients financially and complies with therapeutic equivalence standards. What is the PRIMARY fraud classification?
options: A. No fraud occurred, as therapeutic equivalence justifies the substitution
B. False claims fraud, as submitting claims for medications not actually dispensed constitutes material misrepresentation to the government payer
C. Only a business practice dispute requiring contract modification, not fraud investigation
D. Fraud only if the generic medications were objectively inferior in clinical outcomes

questions.text: A whistleblower employed by a state education department discloses that administrators falsified standardized test score data for 89 schools to obtain $23 million in performance-based federal education funding. The whistleblower seeks to file a qui tam action under the False Claims Act. Which element would present the GREATEST legal challenge in this case?
options: A. Establishing that false claims were submitted to the federal government, which is clearly documented
B. Demonstrating the whistleblower has direct knowledge of the fraud, which they possess as an employee
C. Proving materiality—that the false test scores were important to the government's payment decision—given that performance metrics are fundamental to funding allocation
D. Meeting the relator's standing requirement, as the whistleblower's employment status provides clear standing
